one_line_takeaway: First dedicated survey of machine-unlearning verification, contributing a behavioral/parametric taxonomy plus a seven-dimension comparison framework and an original adversarial threat model showing that both verification paradigms -- DP/PoUL-based parametric methods via non-invertible-mapping log forgery, and fingerprint/privacy/performance-based behavioral methods via output manipulation -- can be defeated by a dishonest model provider without actually deleting the data.
key_finding: The survey's central original finding is a demonstrated asymmetry and joint vulnerability across both verification paradigms: parametric verification methods (differential privacy, Proof-of-Unlearning/PoUL) offer formal guarantees but are shown, via cited attacks, to be defeatable because the training-data-to-parameters mapping is non-invertible -- a dishonest provider can construct a 'forged' training trajectory using a replacement mini-batch that never touches D_f yet produces parameter updates matching the true trajectory within a small tolerance (epsilon as low as 10^-6 per Thudi et al.; a nearest-neighbor substitution variant from Zhang et al. reliably bypasses verification at a looser practical tolerance of epsilon = 10^-3). Behavioral verification, meanwhile, is characterized as even more fragile since it relies purely on observable outputs decoupled from true internal memory state, making it susceptible to fingerprint suppression, DP/noise-based obfuscation of privacy-leakage signals, and data-poisoning-driven spoofing of performance degradation -- all without any actual data removal. The paper's synthesized conclusion is that no existing verification method (behavioral or parametric) simultaneously satisfies formal guarantees, black-box operability, sample-level granularity, and adversarial robustness, and that reliability and practicality trade off against each other across the entire literature it surveys.
core_contribution: A systematic survey (ACM Computing Surveys, 37 pages) that is, per the authors, the first structured survey dedicated to machine-unlearning VERIFICATION (as opposed to unlearning algorithms themselves). Its own contributions are threefold: (1) a taxonomy splitting verification methods into 'behavioral' (fingerprint, privacy-analysis, model-performance) and 'parametric' (differential-privacy-based, reproducibility/Proof-of-Unlearning-based) categories, built around a formal notation (hypothesis spaces H, H', H_r; verification predicate V(w', D_f)); (2) a seven-dimension comparative framework (Q1 theoretical guarantee, Q2 black-box access requirement, Q3 sample-level granularity, Q4 verification accuracy, Q5 no pre-injected data required, Q6 efficiency/scalability, Q7 method specificity) used to score each method family with symbolic indicators (check/triangle/dot/cross); and (3) an original Section 6 'Threats to Verification Reliability' that defines an explicit adversarial threat model (dishonest model provider with full control of the training pipeline) and synthesizes, from cited attack papers, how verification can be defeated from the parameter space (forging maps / log manipulation) and the behavioral space (fingerprint suppression, privacy-signal obfuscation, performance spoofing). Section 7 closes with the authors' own list of open problems (unified definition of forgetting, reliability under practical constraints, generalization across samples, verification under data duplication, hierarchical hybrid verification).
threat_model: Defined explicitly by the authors in Section 6.1: an external verifier/auditor attempts to determine whether a model has forgotten a designated subset D_f after an unlearning protocol claims to have been invoked. The model provider is assumed potentially dishonest, with full control over the training pipeline -- able to manipulate training data, alter update trajectories, falsify audit logs, or fine-tune the final model specifically to pass verification checks -- while still claiming D_f has been unlearned. Two attack surfaces follow from this model: (a) parameter-space threats, where the provider forges training logs/trajectories that satisfy parametric verification (e.g., Proof-of-Unlearning) without actually deleting D_f, exploiting the non-unique (non-invertible) mapping between training data and final parameters; (b) behavioral-space threats, where the provider manipulates observable model outputs to pass fingerprint, privacy-leakage, or performance-based checks while covertly retaining the forgotten data's influence. This is the paper's own synthesized threat model, not attributed to a single cited work, though it draws its concrete attack mechanics from Thudi et al. and Zhang et al. (both cited, not the authors' own experiments).
limitations: As stated or clearly implied by the authors themselves: (1) the survey identifies but does not resolve the absence of a unified, operational definition of 'forgetting' across the field, meaning the comparisons it draws (e.g., via Q1-Q7 and Table 4) inherit whatever definitional inconsistency exists in the underlying literature; (2) cryptographic/TEE-hardened defenses against the forging attacks they describe (SGX-based approaches, SNARK+hash-chain schemes) are noted by the authors as carrying 'significant computational and deployment overhead' that 'limits their practicality in large-scale or resource-constrained settings' -- i.e., the mitigations the survey covers are not shown to be deployable at scale; (3) the authors explicitly flag that most surveyed verification methods rely on idealized assumptions (stationary data distributions, full model access, deterministic/reproducible training) that 'rarely hold in real-world deployments'; (4) as a survey, the paper performs no independent empirical validation of the attacks or defenses it describes -- all quantitative figures (e.g., the epsilon tolerances) are taken from the cited primary-source papers, not reproduced or verified by these authors.

quantitative_results: Forging-map attack (Thudi et al., cited, not this paper's own experiment): forged parameter-update deviation epsilon as low as 10^-6 still passes verification.
Input-space nearest-neighbor substitution attack (Zhang et al., cited): forged trajectories reliably bypass verification under a practical tolerance threshold of epsilon = 10^-3.
baselines_compared: Table 1 comparison against 19 prior related surveys (refs [53],[62],[64],[78],[82],[91],[109],[134],[34],[86],[71],[87],[138],[121],[88],[70],[12],[72],[65]) across seven coverage dimensions -- this paper claims full coverage on all seven where every prior survey has at most partial coverage on any single dimension.
